title: Package travel and linked travel arrangements: make the protection of travellers more effective and simplify and clarify certain aspects

scope_definitions.description: Clarifying and potentially extending the definitions of 'package travel' and 'linked travel arrangements' to address evolving online sales models and ensure consistent application across the EU.
scope_definitions.salience: Industry (especially online travel agencies, aggregators) wants clarity and predictable liability, fearing over-regulation or broad interpretations. Consumer groups want broader scope to ensure protection regardless of how services are booked, while Member States seek clear rules for effective national enforcement.

financial_protection_insolvency.description: Strengthening and harmonising financial protection requirements for organisers and retailers, particularly concerning insolvency, and clarifying the scope of protection (e.g., for airline insolvency).
financial_protection_insolvency.salience: Consumer groups demand robust, harmonised protection for all payments and repatriation. The travel industry (especially smaller tour operators, airlines) expresses concerns about the cost and administrative burden of additional financial guarantees. Member States may resist full harmonisation if it conflicts with existing national schemes.

reimbursement_refund_timelines.description: Establishing clearer rules and stricter timelines for the reimbursement of travellers in cases of cancellations or non-performance, and addressing the issue of cash versus voucher refunds.
reimbursement_refund_timelines.salience: Consumer organisations advocate for quick cash refunds as the default, drawing lessons from the COVID-19 pandemic. The travel industry (organisers, agents) often prefers the flexibility to offer vouchers (especially during crises) to manage liquidity, and may push for longer timelines or conditions for cash refunds.

allocation_of_liability.description: Redefining and clarifying the responsibilities and liability between organisers, retailers, and service providers, especially in complex online booking scenarios and for linked travel arrangements.
allocation_of_liability.salience: Different segments of the travel industry (e.g., traditional tour operators vs. online platforms) seek clear, fair allocation of liability to avoid being held responsible for components they don't directly control. Consumer groups want clear lines of responsibility to ensure effective redress for travellers, regardless of the booking chain.

information_requirements.description: Adjusting the type, clarity, and timing of pre-contractual and contractual information provided to travellers to ensure transparency without creating excessive information overload.
information_requirements.salience: Consumer groups demand comprehensive, unambiguous information to empower travellers. The travel industry fears excessive, prescriptive information requirements that burden sales processes and may lead to 'information fatigue' among consumers, hindering effective communication.

unavoidable_extraordinary_circumstances.description: Revising provisions related to Unavoidable and Extraordinary Circumstances (UECs), particularly concerning the rights to refund, re-routing, and compensation during crises like pandemics or natural disasters.
unavoidable_extraordinary_circumstances.salience: Consumer groups want clear, strong rights for travellers even in UECs. The travel industry seeks flexibility and recognition of the financial strain UECs impose, potentially advocating for shared burden or state support, and clearer definitions of what constitutes UECs to avoid ambiguity.

enforcement_cross_border_cooperation.description: Enhancing the effectiveness of enforcement mechanisms and cross-border cooperation between national authorities to ensure consistent application and redress across the EU single market.
enforcement_cross_border_cooperation.salience: National consumer authorities and consumer groups want stronger, harmonised enforcement powers and better cooperation. The industry wants predictable and fair enforcement without disproportionate penalties, potentially advocating for alternative dispute resolution. Member States are often keen to retain some level of control over national enforcement processes.

digitalisation_online_platforms.description: Adapting the directive to the digital economy, specifically addressing the role, responsibilities, and liabilities of online platforms, aggregators, and providers of dynamic packaging.
digitalisation_online_platforms.salience: Traditional tour operators and consumer groups often push for online platforms to be held to similar standards to ensure fair competition and consumer protection. Online platforms and digital service providers argue their role is different and existing definitions may not fit, fearing undue regulatory burden that stifles innovation.

proportionality_for_smes.description: Ensuring that the revised rules are proportionate and do not create disproportionate administrative or financial burdens for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in the travel sector.
proportionality_for_smes.salience: SME associations and some Member States advocate for specific flexibilities or reduced requirements for smaller businesses to ensure competitiveness and prevent market exit. Consumer groups argue that the level of traveller protection should not depend on the size of the business, advocating for a level playing field of rights.
